document: Statutory tort for serious invasions of privacy _ OAIC (Australia - Row 24)

sections.heading: Statutory tort for serious invasions of privacy
sections.title: Statutory tort for serious invasions of privacy
sections.summary: This section introduces the statutory tort for serious invasions of privacy as a legal mechanism allowing individuals to seek redress for privacy harms in court. It establishes that the tort applies beyond traditional Privacy Act entities to include individuals and other entities. The framework aims to address current and emerging privacy risks through a flexible approach.

requirements.text: Statutory tort for serious invasions of privacy

sections.source_quote: Statutory tort for serious invasions of privacy | OAIC

sections.heading: Published: 19 June 2025
sections.title: Statutory tort for serious invasions of privacy: legal framework and scope
sections.summary: This section details the statutory tort for serious invasions of privacy, which allows individuals to seek legal redress for privacy harms. It specifies that the tort applies broadly beyond Privacy Act entities, covering individuals and other entities. The tort requires demonstrating that the plaintiff had a reasonable expectation of privacy and that the public interest in privacy outweighs any countervailing public interest.

requirements.text: An individual (plaintiff) may have a cause of action against another person or organisation (defendant) who has invaded their privacy by doing one or more of the following, in instances where the plaintiff would have had a reasonable expectation of privacy in all the circumstances: intruding upon the individual's seclusion – for example, by physically intruding into their private space; misusing information that relates to the plaintiff.
A plaintiff will also be required to demonstrate that the public interest in protecting their privacy outweighs any countervailing public interest.

sections.source_quote: An individual (plaintiff) may have a cause of action against another person or organisation (defendant) who has invaded their privacy by doing one or more of the following, in instances where the plaintiff would have had a reasonable expectation of privacy in all the circumstances: intruding upon the individual's seclusion – for example, by physically intruding into their private space; misusing information that relates to the plaintiff.

sections.heading: Timeframes
sections.title: Timeframes for statutory tort proceedings
sections.summary: This section outlines the timeframes for initiating statutory tort proceedings for serious invasions of privacy. If the plaintiff is under 18 at the time of the alleged invasion, proceedings must commence before their 21st birthday. Otherwise, the timeframe is the earlier of one year after the plaintiff became aware of the invasion or three years after the invasion occurred.

requirements.text: If a plaintiff is under 18 years of age when the alleged invasion of privacy occurred, proceedings must commence before their 21st birthday.
In other circumstances, the timeframe is the earlier of: 1 year after the day on which the plaintiff became aware of the invasion of privacy; and 3 years after the invasion of privacy occurred.

sections.source_quote: Proceedings under the statutory tort must be commenced within certain timeframes. If a plaintiff is under 18 years of age when the alleged invasion of privacy occurred, proceedings must commence before their 21st birthday. In other circumstances, the timeframe is the earlier of: 1 year after the day on which the plaintiff became aware of the invasion of privacy; and 3 years after the invasion of privacy occurred.

sections.heading: Site map
sections.title: Site map and navigation
sections.summary: This section provides a site map for the OAIC website, including links to copyright, terms and conditions, privacy policy, accessibility, and contact information. It does not contain substantive legal content relevant to data transfers or privacy torts.

It does not contain substantive legal content relevant to data transfers or privacy torts.
